perturbation.original_text: WCC, in performing under this Agreement, shall act as and be an independent contractor, and this Agreement is not intended to and does not create in any manner a principal-agent, employer-employee, partnership or joint venture relationship between WCC and XLI. Neither party shall have the right or authority to assume or to create any obligation or responsibility, expressed or implied on behalf or in the name of the other party or to bind the other party in any manner.
perturbation.changed_text: WCC, in performing under this Agreement, shall act as and be an independent contractor, and this Agreement is not intended to and does not create in any manner a principal-agent, employer-employee, partnership or joint venture relationship between WCC and XLI. WCC shall have the right or authority to assume obligations or responsibilities, expressed or implied, on behalf of XLI as reasonably necessary to fulfill the PROMOTION activities outlined in Attachment A.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that WCC, as an independent contractor, has the authority to assume obligations on behalf of XLI, the contract contradicts the standard definition of an independent contractor relationship. Independent contractors typically do not have the power to bind the companies they are contracted with. This creates ambiguity about the true nature of the relationship and whether it could be construed as an agency relationship.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Agency Law - specifically the definition of an independent contractor versus an agent.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification gives the independent contractor (WCC) the power to assume obligations on behalf of the contracting company (XLI). This contradicts the fundamental principle of an independent contractor relationship, where the contractor acts on its own behalf and does not bind the company unless explicit agency is established separately. The change introduces elements of an agency relationship, potentially blurring the lines and creating liability issues.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . [Last reviewed in October of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS trial process/advocacy COMMERCE LIFE EVENTS family & personal matters elder law juvenile law wills PROPERTY trusts inheritances & estates THE LEGAL PROCESS class actions courts criminal procedure legal practice/ethics wex articles business law agency wex definitions business sectors civil procedure family law individual rights legal education and practice property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the definition of an independent contractor relationship by granting WCC the authority to assume obligations on behalf of XLI, potentially creating an agency relationship. The original text explicitly states that neither party has the right to assume obligations on behalf of the other. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent.' The modification grants WCC this authority, thus contradicting the initial independent contractor agreement.

perturbation.original_text: WCC shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless XLI, and its respective affiliates, officers, directors, employees, agents and representatives, from any and all claims, losses, damages, expenses, costs and other liabilities to any person or entity ("Claims") arising out of, relating to or in connection with: (i) the breach by WCC of any of the representations and warranties made by WCC in this Agreement or the failure by WCC to fulfill any of its covenants set forth herein, and (ii) the use by XLI (as approved by WCC) of the WCC Trademarks pursuant to Section 1.1.1, above. Notwithstanding any other provision herein, under no circumstances shall WCC be liable for any claims arising out of the negligent acts or omissions of XLI or third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: WCC shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless XLI, and its respective affiliates, officers, directors, employees, agents and representatives, from any and all claims, losses, damages, expenses, costs and other liabilities to any person or entity ("Claims") arising out of, relating to or in connection with: (i) the breach by WCC of any of the representations and warranties made by WCC in this Agreement or the failure by WCC to fulfill any of its covenants set forth herein, and (ii) the use by XLI of the WCC Trademarks pursuant to Section 1.1.1, above. Notwithstanding any other provision herein, WCC shall only be liable for claims equal to 10% of the total agreement fee.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a standard indemnification clause where WCC indemnifies XLI. The modification removes the requirement that XLI's use of WCC's trademarks be 'as approved by WCC' for WCC to indemnify XLI. This creates a situation where WCC could be liable even for unapproved uses of its trademarks by XLI. The modification then adds a limitation on liability, capping WCC's liability at 10% of the total agreement fee. This contradicts general indemnification principles, which aim to make the indemnified party whole, and may be unenforceable depending on the jurisdiction and the nature of the claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Indemnification Principles and limitations on liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the 'as approved by WCC' condition and capping liability, the modification contradicts typical indemnification principles that seek to fully compensate the indemnified party for losses. Indemnification usually covers all reasonably foreseeable damages, and the cap introduced here significantly limits this coverage, potentially rendering the indemnification clause ineffective and violating the purpose of indemnification laws, which is to shift the full risk to the indemnifying party.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a limitation on liability for WCC, capping it at 10% of the agreement fee, and removes the 'as approved by WCC' clause. This contradicts the general principle of indemnification, which aims to fully compensate the indemnified party (XLI) for losses. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that indemnification usually covers all reasonably foreseeable damages, and the introduced cap significantly limits this coverage, potentially making the indemnification clause ineffective. While the scraped snippets are not helpful, the Law Explanation highlights the core purpose of indemnification laws is to shift the full risk to the indemnifying party. The modified contract's liability cap undermines this purpose, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a conflict between said Sponsor Benefits Schedule and this Agreement, the Sponsor Benefits Schedule shall control.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a conflict between said Sponsor Benefits Schedule and this Agreement, this Agreement shall control.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause establishes that the Sponsor Benefits Schedule takes precedence over the main agreement in case of conflict. The modified clause reverses this priority, giving the main agreement precedence. This has a huge impact on what the contract considers important. This is a contradiction because if the Sponsor Benefits Schedule has conditions that change the actual agreement, it has been removed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract interpretation and order of precedence.
perturbation.law_explanation: Reversing the order of precedence directly contradicts standard contract interpretation principles, particularly when specific schedules or exhibits are designed to elaborate on or modify the general terms of the agreement. Parties often intend for such schedules to take precedence in case of conflict because they contain more specific and tailored provisions. This change could invalidate specific agreements outlined in the schedule, making them unenforceable and leading to disputes over the parties' obligations. It goes against established legal understanding of how agreements with supplementary documents should be interpreted.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts established contract interpretation principles. By reversing the order of precedence between the main agreement and the Sponsor Benefits Schedule, the change undermines the purpose of having a schedule designed to elaborate on or modify the general terms. The Law Explanation states this change could invalidate agreements outlined in the schedule, making them unenforceable. While no scraped law snippets were provided, the principle that specific schedules take precedence due to their tailored provisions is a commonly understood tenet of contract law, and the changed text directly reverses this precedence.
